content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 30, 2017

Kelly White
Vice President and Regional Counsel
D.R. Horton, Inc.
11241 Slater Avenue NE, Suite 120
Kirkland, WA 98033

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-107

Dear Ms. White:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of D.R. Horton, Inc. regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

If D.R. Horton, Inc. pays the costs of a special election in the City of Chino pursuant to an agreement with the City, would the payment of those costs would be a reportable payment under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Yes. D.R. Horton, Inc.'s payment of the special election costs would be reportable under the Act based on the facts presented.

FACTS

Chino Municipal Code Section 20.15.020 prohibits the City Council from increasing the density of residential real property within the City without voter approval. In accordance with that provision, D.R. Horton, Inc. ("D.R. Horton"), a residential developer, recently requested that the City hold a special election on a ballot measure, titled "Measure H," that would amend the City's General Plan to authorize up to 180 single-family units on 30 acres of residential real property currently authorized for no more than one unit per acre.

The City agreed to hold the special election if D.R. Horton paid its costs, the cost to implement the project, and the cost to ameliorate the impacts of the increased housing density that

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-107
Page No. 2

would result if Measure H is approved. The estimated cost of the special election is $250,000. The
City has scheduled the special election for July 11, 2017.

A committee has been formed to support the passage of Measure H. The members of the
committee are not D.R. Horton employees. You understand them to be City residents who support
this project. D.R. Horton intends to make a contribution to this committee which will qualify D.R.
Horton as a major donor. As such, D.R. Horton will be required to complete and file Form 461. You
are the authorized representative of D.R. Horton.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines the terms "contribution" and "expenditure" in part as payments for political
purposes. (Sections 82015 and 82025.) Any person who makes a payment for political purposes
may have campaign disclosure obligations under the Act. (Sections 82013, 82013, and 82025.) A
payment is made for political purposes if it is intended to influence the action of voters for or
against the qualification or passage of any measure. (Regulations 18215 and 18225.) A business
entity's payment of the costs of a special election is for political purposes. (Bagatelos Advice
Letter, No. A-95-238.)

Considering the context of D.R. Horton's agreement to pay the City for the costs of the
special election, that agreement and the payment itself are both inextricably linked to D.R. Horton's
overall efforts to enact Measure H. Therefore, D.R. Horton's payment of the special election costs
to the City would be for political purposes.

Because D.R. Horton has agreed to pay the special election costs directly to the City and
that payment would be for political purposes, we conclude that the payment would be reportable
under the Act based on the facts presented. You would report this payment on D.R. Horton's Form
461.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl
